si.t: 1. Phishing Attack Leading to Credential Compromise
Attackers trick employees into revealing login credentials
Lack of phishing awareness; no multi-factor authentication (MFA)
A.7.2.2: Information security awareness, education, and training; A.9.4.1: Management of secret authentication information (implement MFA); A.12.2.1: Protection against malware (email filtering)
Outdated antivirus software; lack of regular backups
A.12.2.1: Protection against malware (updated antivirus); A.12.3.1: Information backup; A.12.6.1: Management of technical vulnerabilities (patch management)
3. Data Breach via Compromised Third-Party Vendor
Attackers exploit vendor’s weak security to access company data
No vendor security assessment; excessive data sharing
A.15.1.1: Information security in supplier relationships; A.15.1.2: Addressing security within supplier agreements; A.13.2.4: Confidentiality or non-disclosure agreements
4. Insider Threat Leading to Data Theft
Excessive access privileges; lack of monitoring
A.9.2.3: Management of privileged access rights; A.12.4.1: Event logging; A.7.1.1: Screening (background checks)
5. Denial of Service (DoS) Attack Disrupting Web Services
Attack overwhelms web server, causing downtime
No DoS protection mechanisms in place
A.13.1.1: Network controls (DoS protection measures); A.13.1.3: Segregation in networks (use of WAF); A.16.1.5: Response to information security incidents
6. Loss of Data Due to Natural Disaster
Flood or fire destroys physical hardware
No disaster recovery plan; data not backed up offsite

A.17.1.1: Planning information security continuity; A.17.2.1: Availability of information processing facilities (offsite backups); A.17.1.3: Verify, review, and evaluate information security continuity
Weak password policy; no two-factor authentication
A.9.2.1: User access management; A.9.4.1: Management of secret authentication information (implement MFA); A.13.1.1: Network controls (firewalls)
Data breach leading to loss of customer data
Misconfigured access controls; lack of encryption
A.9.2.3: Management of privileged access rights; A.10.1.1: Policy on the use of cryptographic controls (encrypt data); A.12.4.1: Event logging (security audits)
Unencrypted hard drives; no device tracking
A.8.2.3: Handling of assets (implement encryption); A.9.4.1: Management of secret authentication information; A.11.2.6: Secure disposal or reuse of equipment (remote wipe capabilities)
No antivirus software; users lack security awareness
A.12.2.1: Protection against malware; A.7.2.2: Information security awareness, education, and training; A.12.1.3: Capacity management (email filtering)
A.9.2.1: User access management (strong password policy); A.9.4.1: Management of secret authentication information (implement MFA); A.7.2.2: Information security awareness, education, and training
Key Requirements, Documents Needed, and Evidence
Determine external and internal issues relevant to the ISMS objectives
Documented analysis of internal and external issues
- Meeting minutes or records - Stakeholder analysis reports
Identify stakeholders and their information security requirements
List of interested parties and their requirements
Define the boundaries and applicability of the ISMS
- Approved ISMS scope statement - Organizational charts
Establish, implement, maintain, and continually improve the ISMS
- Process maps - Evidence of ISMS implementation
Top management must demonstrate leadership and commitment to the ISMS
- Meeting minutes with top management - Signed statements - Communication records
Establish an information security policy aligned with the organization's objectives
- Signed information security policy - Communication to staff
Assign and communicate responsibilities for information security roles
- Organizational charts with ISMS roles - Job descriptions - Communication records
Plan actions to address risks and opportunities
- Records showing planning activities - Risk assessment schedules
Establish and implement a risk assessment process
- Risk assessment methodology - Risk assessment reports/risk register
Implement a risk treatment process and select controls
- Risk treatment plan - Statement of Applicability (SoA)
- Approved risk treatment plans - Updated SoA
Set measurable information security objectives and plans
- Information security objectives - Plans to achieve objectives
- Monitoring and measurement records - Meeting minutes discussing objectives
Determine and provide necessary resources for the ISMS
Ensure personnel are competent through education, training, or experience
Personnel must be aware of the information security policy, their contributions, and implications of non-compliance
Establish internal and external communications relevant to the ISMS
Control the creation, updating, and control of documented information
- Document control procedures - Master list of documents and records
- Controlled documents with versioning - Access logs
Plan, implement, and control the processes needed to meet ISMS requirements
- Logs of operational activities - Change requests and approvals
Perform risk assessments at planned intervals
- Updated risk assessments - Risk assessment reports
- Evidence of implemented controls - Monitoring reports
Determine what needs to be monitored and measured, methods, and analyze results
Conduct internal audits at planned intervals
- Completed audit checklists - Audit findings and corrective actions
Top management reviews the ISMS at planned intervals
- Management review procedure - Management review meeting minutes
- Action items from management reviews - Decisions made
Address nonconformities and take action to prevent recurrence
- Nonconformity and corrective action procedure - Corrective action records
- Nonconformity reports - Records of corrective actions
Enhance the suitability, adequacy, and effectiveness of the ISMS
Core ISMS requirement, sets the foundation for information security governance
Defines accountability and responsibilities, crucial for ISMS effectiveness
Prevents conflicts of interest, important for protecting data integrity
Ensures senior management supports ISMS, essential for overall effectiveness
Required for regulatory compliance and incident response, especially in case of data breaches
Maintains awareness of industry developments and threat intelligence
Provides up-to-date threat data, essential in a consulting environment
Ensures security is considered in all client-related projects
Inventory of information and other associated assets
Required for identifying and managing assets, especially in cloud-based and remote environments
Acceptable use of information and other associated assets
Ensures employees handle information properly, critical in data handling and consulting
Necessary for managing equipment (e.g., laptops) and access rights during employee offboarding
Helps in prioritizing protection of sensitive client and internal information
Important for identifying sensitive information in cloud storage or shared drives
Ensures secure information sharing, especially relevant in client interactions
Fundamental for controlling access to sensitive data and systems
Ensures secure authentication practices, crucial in a remote work environment
Protects credentials and authentication mechanisms from misuse
Ensures only authorized users can access sensitive client and corporate data
Important for managing security with cloud providers and third-party vendors
Addressing information security within supplier agreements
Ensures cloud providers and partners meet required security standards
Managing information security in the ICT supply chain
Important if third-party tools/software are used in service delivery
Monitoring, review and change management of supplier services
Ensures suppliers adhere to required security standards as DamnArt
Information security for use of cloud services
Ensures tools/software used in client services meet security requirements
Information security incident management planning and preparation
Ensures readiness to manage security incidents effectively, prote4cting client's data and organizational integrity
Assessment and decision on information security events
Enables timely evaluation and response to security events, minimizing client impact
Ensures incidents are managed in an organized manner, reducing operational and reputational risks
Supports continual improvement by learning from past incidents
Supports investigation and compliance requirements through proper evidence handling
Ensures security remains effective during disruptions, supporting business continuity
Ensures systems are prepared for disruptions and service continuity
Legal, statutory, regulatory and contractual requirements
Ensures compliance with laws and data protection requirements
Protects proprietary content and methodologies used in consulting
